Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a consistent study routine. I'd tell myself I was going to sit down and work through everything, but then I'd get distracted or just feel overwhelmed by how much there was. I'd end up scrolling through the course materials without really absorbing anything. Some weeks I'd put in a lot of hours just staring at readings, but I wasn't actually getting anywhere meaningful with it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then panic when I realized something was due and try to cram. It wasn't really tied to deadlines in a productive way—more like I'd notice a deadline had passed and feel awful about it. My extracurriculars and other stuff always seemed to take priority even when I knew I should be studying.
Q3: Usually fear, to be honest. I'd log in when I got that sinking feeling that I was falling behind or when I'd get a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to see how bad things had gotten, like checking the damage. It was rarely because I was excited to learn something new.
Q4: Not really. I tried to make it a habit early on, like checking in every morning, but that fell apart within the first few weeks. The LMS just felt like this overwhelming space with too many things I hadn't done. Every time I logged in, I'd see all these incomplete tasks and it made me want to close the tab immediately.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually kind of helpful when I participated because hearing from other students made me feel less alone. Some of the reading materials were good too—I prefer reading and writing over videos. When I actually engaged with the written content, I felt like I was learning something, but that didn't happen as often as it should have.
Q6: I barely watched the videos. I'd start them and then my mind would wander, or I'd get anxious about how long they were. I also ignored a lot of the optional practice exercises because they felt pointless if I wasn't going to do the actual assignments anyway. It was like, why bother practicing if I've already fallen too far behind?
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to something I needed to submit, or if it was short enough that I could actually finish it. Anything that felt like it would take hours just made me shut down. I needed quick wins, but the course didn't really offer many of those.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford when I was already drowning in required stuff I hadn't done. Required ones carried this weight of obligation that made me anxious, but optional ones just got pushed aside entirely. It's weird because I might have actually enjoyed some of the optional stuff if I hadn't been so stressed about everything else.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of PDFs and readings to my laptop thinking I'd read them later without being online. I also have notebooks where I'd write out concepts trying to understand them. Sometimes I'd look things up on YouTube or other websites because the course explanations weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were nights where I'd sit with my printed notes and textbook for hours, trying to make sense of things, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts with friends from my extracurriculars who were in different programs—just trying to explain things out loud to see if I understood. The LMS had no idea any of that was happening.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a reading, skim it in the LMS, then close everything and read it properly on my tablet later. Or I'd save lecture slides and review them on the bus. The problem is I'd convince myself I'd study them later and then sometimes I just... wouldn't. But when I did, it wasn't tracked anywhere.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart. I had stuff going on with my extracurriculars that was really demanding, and I was scared about how poorly I was doing. The fear kind of paralyzed me instead of motivating me. I'd open the LMS, see all the red marks and incomplete assignments, and just freeze up completely.
Q13: Absolutely. The workload felt impossible when I was already behind, and being confused about the material made it worse. I was tired all the time from trying to balance everything, and when I had to choose between course work and other responsibilities, the course kept losing. The LMS became this place I dreaded visiting.
Q14: So many times. I'd scroll through materials, click on things, maybe watch a few minutes of a video, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I was going through the motions without actually processing anything. It was like I was trying to convince myself I was studying when I really wasn't.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. I think I was scared of failing at them, so not doing them felt safer somehow? Like if I didn't try, I couldn't really fail. It's messed up logic, but that's where my head was at. The anxiety just made me avoid everything.
Q16: Deadlines made me panic rather than plan. I'd see a deadline approaching and feel this wave of dread, then either scramble to do something last minute or just let it pass and feel terrible about it. I rarely used deadlines to pace myself—they were more like these looming threats that I couldn't escape from.
Q17: I didn't get much feedback because I wasn't submitting much work. When I did get feedback, it was usually too late for me to do anything about it, or I was too scared to read it carefully. The peer support stuff helped a little—having someone check in made me feel like someone noticed I existed. But it wasn't enough to turn things around.
Q18: The course structure felt really rigid and unforgiving. Once I fell behind, there was no way to catch up that felt manageable. Everything built on everything else, so missing early stuff meant I was lost for later stuff. I needed more flexibility or checkpoints where I could reset, but it just kept moving forward without me.
Q19: The sheer number of things to keep track of made engagement harder. Nineteen online courses worth of content is a lot, and this one got lost in the shuffle. Having clearer priorities or a simpler structure would have helped. Also, more low-stakes activities early on might have built my confidence instead of making me feel immediately overwhelmed.
Q20: I think that was me, honestly. I'd log in, click around, watch bits of videos, read discussion posts—all this activity that looked like engagement but wasn't producing any actual work. Sometimes you're just treading water, trying to feel like you're doing something when you're really just scared and lost.
Q21: That wasn't my experience at all—I had low activity AND performed poorly. But I guess some people are just more efficient or they're learning in ways that don't require the LMS. They might have strong study skills or outside resources that work better for them. I wish I could have figured that out.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything or how you were feeling while you did it. They miss all the offline studying, the anxiety spirals, the conversations with peers outside the system. They're like a shadow of what's really happening.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling like you can succeed at it. It's not just showing up or clicking through things—it's being mentally present and invested. I wanted to feel that way, but fear and stress kept getting in the way. Real engagement requires feeling safe enough to try.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how scared I was all the time, or how much I actually wanted to do well even though my actions didn't reflect that. It wouldn't show the hours I spent worrying about the course without actually working on it, or how the peer support conversations helped me feel less alone even if they didn't fix my grades. The emotional reality is completely invisible.
